coredata.dc:title: Real-time and specific monitoring of nitric oxide and evaluating of the oxidative stress in living cells and zebrafish under the pollutant exposure using a carbon dot-based composite fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Meng
Tong, Changlun
coredata.dc:description: Nitric oxide (NO) functions as an essential signalling molecule in various physiological and pathological pathways. In vitro and vivo redox processes mediated by reactive oxygen species (ROS) and nitric oxide (NO) directly influence the intracellular state. In this study, a red-emitting fluorescent nanoprobe, N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, was synthesized to monitor NO fluctuations in living cells and zebrafish under the exposure to various pollutants. Red-emissive carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and urea as carbon / nitrogen sources, and H2SO4 as sulfur source. Glutathione (GSH) was introduced to link N,S-CDs with metal organic complexes (Zn-ICA) through an amidation reaction to fabricate a carbon dot-based composite fluorescent probe, which greatly improved the selectivity, stability, and response time of the N,S-CDs. The composite probe has high selectivity and sensitivity with limit of detection (LOD) of 96.0 nM. Furthermore, the proposed probe was successfully used to monitor the dynamic changes in NO levels and evaluate oxidative stress in MCF-7 cells and zebrafish under the exposure to various pollutants, including seven heavy metal ions (such as Pb2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+) and nine organic pollutants at different concentrations and exposure times. This work provides a novel strategy for constructing highly selective and red-emitting fluorescent probe for real-time and dynamic monitoring of NO and further evaluating oxidative stress induced by pollutants in vitro and in vivo via fluorescence imaging.

head.ce:title: Real-time and specific monitoring of nitric oxide and evaluating of the oxidative stress in living cells and zebrafish under the pollutant exposure using a carbon dot-based composite fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Ecological Health, Ministry of Education, College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310058, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Ecological Health, Ministry of Education, College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Ecological Health, Ministry of Education, College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310058, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitric oxide (NO) functions as an essential signalling molecule in various physiological and pathological pathways. In vitro and vivo redox processes mediated by reactive oxygen species (ROS) and nitric oxide (NO) directly influence the intracellular state. In this study, a red-emitting fluorescent nanoprobe, N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, was synthesized to monitor NO fluctuations in living cells and zebrafish under the exposure to various pollutants. Red-emissive carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and urea as carbon / nitrogen sources, and H2SO4 as sulfur source. Glutathione (GSH) was introduced to link N,S-CDs with metal organic complexes (Zn-ICA) through an amidation reaction to fabricate a carbon dot-based composite fluorescent probe, which greatly improved the selectivity, stability, and response time of the N,S-CDs. The composite probe has high selectivity and sensitivity with limit of detection (LOD) of 96.0 nM. Furthermore, the proposed probe was successfully used to monitor the dynamic changes in NO levels and evaluate oxidative stress in MCF-7 cells and zebrafish under the exposure to various pollutants, including seven heavy metal ions (such as Pb2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+) and nine organic pollutants at different concentrations and exposure times. This work provides a novel strategy for constructing highly selective and red-emitting fluorescent probe for real-time and dynamic monitoring of NO and further evaluating oxidative stress induced by pollutants in vitro and in vivo via fluorescence imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that nitric oxide (NO), a typical pollutant from industrial and domestic waste gases, can cause varying degrees of respiratory damage [1]. However, endogenous NO, which is an active free radical, is also an important gas signalling molecule in organisms, and is ubiquitous in the human immune, nervous, and cardiovascular systems [2]. Generally, endogenous NO, which is the central molecule of reactive nitrogen species (RNS), is produced from l-arginine through nitric oxide synthase (NOS), and its concentration normally increases under the oxidative stress, which further results in an increase in highly reactive nitrogen species (RNS), such as peroxynitrite, and can even lead to cell damage [3,4]. In the context of cellular immune defense, NO also serves as a typical antioxidant, and can effectively remove excess lipid free radicals and reactive oxygen species (ROS) [5–7]. Furthermore, owing to their high toxicity and bioaccumulation, long-term exposure to low concentration of heavy metal ions and endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) can lead to impaired function and various diseases; these processes are mainly induced by oxidative stress signalling pathways [8]. Nitric oxide has the dual effects of nitrosylation and reduction, but the details of its specific production and functional mechanisms in the exposure process are not yet clear [9]. Hence, it is crucial to develop a highly selective and sensitive method for real-time monitoring of intracellular NO fluctuations in vivo to clarify the biological mechanism of pollutant-induced oxidative damage.

At present, most reported analytical methods for NO such as electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) [10], electrochemistry [11], colorimetry [12], chemiluminescence [13], and mid-infrared absorption spectroscopy [14], have focused on detecting NO in aqueous solution and the gas phase; these approaches have been applied for the diagnosis of respiratory diseases using the fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FENO) [15]. However, the above methods are difficult to apply for the in situ and real-time visual monitoring NO in living organisms. Fluorescent probes can enable the visual monitoring of dynamic changes in NO levels in organisms with high sensitivity, simplicity, rapid response, and imaging properties [16,17]. However, the development of fluorescent probes for NO has been only focused on simple imaging analysis of endogenous and exogenous NO, and they are rarely used in cell exposure or disease models due to their photobleaching properties, poor biocompatibility, and high toxicity. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest owing to their exceptional properties, which include easy preparation and surface modification, high water solubility, low or no toxicity, biocompatibility, and unique optical and chemical stability [18–21]. To date, few CDs have been developed for monitoring NO in vivo; moreover, they present drawbacks such as short excitation and emission wavelengths [22–24], poor tissue penetration and weak anti-interference ability again reactive species in the physiological environment, which is unfavourable for their application in vivo or in living cells [25,26]. Fortunately, metal organic complexes, which have the advantages of high specific surface area, regulable microporosity, and self-assembly, can be loaded with fluorescent substances to adjust the aggregation state of the fluorescent fragments, promote specific interactions, and selectively adsorb target analytes [27–29]. Thus, the combination of carbon dots and metal complexes can effectively construct fluorescent nanohybrid materials and improve the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots. Additionally, in the context of pollution-induced oxidative stress, the total production of ROS is typically detected and evaluated [30–32]. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports of the detection and evaluation of individual ROS species under the exposure to pollutants.

In the present work, our objective was to develop a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and specific detection of NO and to evaluate the NO levels in the context of oxidative stress under the exposure to pollutants, including heavy metal ions and organic pollutants. Red-emissive carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and urea as carbon / nitrogen sources, and H2SO4 as sulfur source, which results in red-emissive carbon dots. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the N,S-CDs are 540 nm and 612 nm, respectively. Glutathione (GSH) was introduced to link the N,S-CDs with metal organic complexes through an amidation reaction ( Scheme 1) to fabricate a metal organic complex fluorescent probe, N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, which greatly improved the selectivity, stability, and response time of the N,S-CDs. NO reacts specifically with the o-phenylenediamine group to generate a benzotriazole structure, which causes fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe and a blue shift in the emission peak. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe was successfully used to monitor the dynamic changes in NO levels and evaluate the oxidative stress state in MCF-7 cells and zebrafish under the exposure to various pollutants, including seven heavy metal ions (Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cr(Ⅵ)) and nine organic pollutants at different concentrations and exposure times. This work provides a novel strategy for constructing highly selective and red-emissive fluorescent probes for real-time and dynamic monitoring of NO and further evaluates the oxidative stress induced by pollutants in vitro and vivo via fluorescent imaging.

Details of the chemicals and instrumentation, preparation of N,S-CDs, synthesis of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, cell viability assay, and colorimetric assay of nitric oxide radicals can be found in the Supporting Information.

Sodium nitroprusside (SNP), which is commonly used as a donor of nitric oxide free radicals, constantly releases NO in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. A general procedure for the fluorescence assay of NO is as follows. 12 μL of 10.0 g/L N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA solution, 200 μL PBS buffer solution (200.0 mM, pH=7.5) and 334 μL ethanol were added to a 2.0 mL test tube. A known concentration of SNP (0.25 −100.0 μM) was then added. The mixture solution was diluted to 2.0 mL, mixed completely and incubated for 5 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence intensity at 612 nm was measured at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. The width of the excitation and emission slits was 5/5 nm. For the selectivity test, possible coexisting interfering substances, such as common metal ions, anions, and other active species, were added to replace the SNP, and the same assay procedure was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of exogenous and endogenous NO in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorescence imaging of exogenous NO in MCF-7 cells, the cells were evenly seeded in 24-well plates (5 ×104 per well) and incubated overnight until adherent. Medium containing N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA (100.0 mg/L) was added to each well, and the cells were continuously cultured for 2 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, medium containing 40.0 μM SNP was added to further incubate the cells for 30 min, and the cells were then divided into three groups and further cultured in the fresh DMEM medium for 0 h, 6 h or 12 h. Fluorescence imaging was performed using an inverted fluorescence microscope after washing the cells twice with PBS buffer. After every replacement of the medium, the cells were washed twice with PBS.

For the fluorescence imaging of endogenous NO in MCF-7 cells, the cells were evenly seeded in 24-well plates (5 ×104 per well) and incubated overnight until adherent. Medium containing 100.0 mg/L N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was added to each well and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. After the medium was removed and the cells were washed twice with PBS, they were divided into two groups. In the first group, the MCF-7 cells were incubated in medium containing 5.0 mg/L lipopolysacchatide (LPS) for 2 h. In the second group, the cells were incubated in a medium containing 5.0 mg/L LPS and 500 μM nitroarginine (L-NNA) for 2 h. For every replacement of medium, the cells need be washed twice with PBS buffer. Fluorescence imaging was performed using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the oxidative stress under the pollutant exposure in MCF-7 cells via fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the oxidative stress induced by pollutants, two types of typical pollutants, heavy metal ions (Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cr2O7 2-, Cd2+, Hg2+) and organic pollutants (BPA, PFBS, TBP, NP, E1, CIP, TC, MB, and PNA), were selected for cell exposure experiments. The MCF-7 cells were cultured at 37 °C for 24 h under the exposure to different concentrations (0, 1.0, 10.0 and 100.0 μM) of heavy metal ions and organic pollutants. After the medium was removed and the cells were washed twice with PBS buffer, 100.0 mg/L of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA solution was added to each well and further cultured for 2 h. The cells were observed using an inverted fluorescence microscope after the medium was removed and washed twice with PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the oxidative stress in zebrafish under the pollutant exposure via fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: First, 3-day-old zebrafish were placed in a 24-well plate (4 strips per well). 1.0 mL PTU embryo medium was added to each well and incubated at 28.5 °C for 4 h. The zebrafish were incubated for a further 24 h in the presence of two typical pollutants at different concentrations: Cd2+ (0, 1.0, 10.0 and 100.0 μM) and 2,4,6-tribromophenol (0, 0.02, 0.1 and 0.5 μM). Next, the medium containing the pollutants was removed and the cells were washed twice with PBS. The zebrafish were further incubated with PBS containing 180.0 mg/L N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA at 28.5 °C for 2 h and subsequently observed using an inverted fluorescence microscope after the medium was removed and washed twice with PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization and optical properties of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation strategy for the fluorescent probe (N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA) is shown in Scheme 1. The red-emissive carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using urea and o-phenylenediamine as carbon / nitrogen sources, and H2SO4 as sulfur source. To enhance the luminescence stability and selectivity of the fluorescent probe, the N,S-CDs were covalently linked to ICA-GSH through an amidation reaction using an EDC/NHS crosslinker to form the composite fluorescent nanoprobe N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA.

The morphology of the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the N,S-CDs were uniformly dispersed and spherical with an average diameter of 2.3 nm as indicated in the inset, and lattice fringes image of the N, S-CDs was shown in Fig. S1 (A). Fig. 1 D shows that N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA had a block structure with an irregular shape and a size distribution range of 10–100 nm. Additionally, it can be seen from Fig. 1 B and Fig. 1 C that the morphologies of Zn-ICA and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA were very similar, as was also confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S1 (B), the XRD spectra revealed that the N,S-CDs did not affect the structure of Zn-ICA, which remained structurally amorphous before and after the N,S-CDs were added. To further analyse the elemental composition of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA and confirm the successful connection between the N,S-CDs and Zn-ICA, XPS characterization of both the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was performed. The XPS spectra in Fig. 1 E demonstrate that N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA mainly contained the elements Zn, O, N, C, and S, and the S content was approximately consistent with the sum of N,S-CDs and Zn-ICA (Table S1), indicating that N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was successfully equipped with the N,S-CDs. Specifically, the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum of the N,S-CDs (Fig. S2A) can be fitted by three peaks at 398.5, 399.8, and 400.4 eV, corresponding to C−N, −NH2 and CN, respectively [33]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of Zn-ICA (Fig. S2E) was deconvoluted into two component peaks at 531.2 and 532.2 eV, corresponding to CO and C−OH, respectively [34]. Notably, for the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA ( Fig. S2C ), the −NH2 peak at 399.8 eV disappeared, and the intensity of the C−N bond at 399.3 eV visibly increased compared to that of Zn-ICA. The peak corresponding to C−OH at 532.2 eV for Zn-ICA also disappeared in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA (Fig. S2F ). All the above XPS results indicate that N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was successfully constructed through an amidation reaction between the amino group of the N,S-CDs and the carboxyl group of Zn-ICA.

The optical properties of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA were investigated using fluorescence and UV-Vis spectroscopy. The excitation and emission spectra of the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA are shown in Fig. 1 F. When excited at 540 nm, the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA exhibited maximum emission peaks at 619 and 612 nm, respectively. The slight blue shift may originate from charge transfer or strong electrostatic interactions between the zinc ions/imidazole ligands and the N,S-CDs in the synthesis of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA [35,36]. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe emitted red fluorescence (λem > 600 nm), which has greater application potential in biological imaging due to its deep tissue penetration and effective avoidance of the background interference from spontaneous fluorescence. In addition, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs in N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was stronger than that of the N,S-CDs, possibly because of the hydrophobic environment in which the N,S-CDs were encapsulated in the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA polymer, which is beneficial for fluorescence emission. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N,S-CDs shown in Fig. 2 A, the clear absorption peak at 280 nm is due to the π − π * transition of CC [37]. The absorption peaks at 536 nm and 570 nm are ascribed to the n − π * transitions of C−N/CN and C−O/OC [38], and both disappear in the spectrum of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, suggesting that the N,S-CDs were covalently linked to the Zn-ICA. The strong absorption peaks of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA at 207 and 287 nm correspond to the UV–vis spectrum of ICA, which were attributed to the π − π * transition of CC and the n − π * transition of CO/CN/C−N, respectively [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA for NO detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 B, with the addition of NO (SNP), the fluorescence of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was gradually quenched, and the maximum emission wavelength exhibited a slight blue shift, which was attributed to the formation of a non-luminescent ground-state complex between NO and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA. This inference was confirmed using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2 C). Upon the addition of NO, the intensity of the stretching vibration peak at 742 cm−1 corresponding to the ortho-substituted amino group decreased and was blue-shifted. Moreover, the in-plane stretching vibration peak of −NH2 at 1581 cm⁻¹ disappeared completely and a new stretching vibration peak at 1381 cm⁻¹ attributed to the N = N bond was formed, which indicates that the o-phenylenediamine groups on the surface of carbon dots may specifically react with NO to generate a benzotriazole structure [17,40]. As shown in Fig. 2 D, the absorption peaks of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA were greatly reduced in the presence of NO, further suggesting the formation of the ground- state complex. It can be speculated that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA is mainly due to a ground-state substance with a benzotriazole group produced by the reaction of NO with the o-phenylenediamine group, which would compete with the ground-state N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA to absorb the excitation light and thereby cause the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA [24].

The quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA by NO was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 E, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA in the absence and presence of NO are 3.31 ns and 2.96 ns, respectively. The apparent difference in the fluorescence lifetime indicates that fluorescence quenching can be ascribed to the dynamic quenching mechanism. In general, fluorescence quenching is caused by dynamic quenching, static quenching, or both [41,42], and can be described theoretically using the Stern-Volmer equation (SV) [43]. The static quenching or dynamic quenching, the plot of F
0
/F (or τ
0
/τ) versus the concentration of the quencher (Q) should be a straight line according to the SV equation. As shown in Fig. 2F, the SV plot of F
0
/F at 612 nm against the SNP concentration is clearly not a straight line. However, it is fully consistent with the modified SV equation F
0
/F= 1 + (Kd+Ks) [Q]+ KdKs [Q]2 (where Kd is the dynamic quenching constant, Ks is the static quenching constant, and Q is the quencher), indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is initiated by both static and dynamic quenching. The values of F
0
/F and the τ0/τ response to the SNP concentration are unequal; the value of F
0
/F (2.196) is much larger than that of τ0/τ (1.108) in the presence of 20.0 μM SNP, indicating that the static quenching is the predominant mechanism [44].

The detection conditions were optimized, and the results are shown in the Supporting Information. The sensing performance of the fluorescent probe for NO was investigated under the determined optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity at 612 nm decreased gradually with increasing concentration of SNP, whereas the maximum emission peak of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA showed a slight blue shift. Fig. 3 B demonstrates the good linearity between the quenching efficiency (F
0 −F)/F
0 and SNP concentration in the range of 0.25 − 25.0 μM. The regression equation is (F
0 −F)/F
0 = 0.02534 [SNP] + 0.02636 (R2 =0.9902), and the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/K) is 96.0 nM. As shown in Table S2 , the sensitivity of the proposed method for NO detection was superior to that of most previously reported methods. In addition, NO is widely distributed in various tissues of organisms; both low concentrations (from picomolar to nanomolar range) and high concentrations (in the micromolar range) are involved in various physiological functions such as cardiovascular regulation, neuromodulation, and immune surveillance [45]. SNP is commonly used as a donor of nitric oxide free radicals, as it constantly releases NO in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. In fact, a micromolar concentration of SNP can continuously release a nanomolar concentration of NO over a period of time [46]. The above results indicate that the sensitivity of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA for NO detection meets the requirements for real-time monitoring of endogenous NO in the cells.

Good selectivity and anti-interference ability are vital for the practical applications of fluorescent probes. To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, the fluorescence responses of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA to possible interferents in the cells, including common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl-, HCO3 -, F-, NO3 -, CO3 2-, SO4 2-), antioxidants (AA, GSH, l-Cys, Glu) and ROS/RNS (ONOO-, H2O2,·OH, 1O2, HOCl), were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 C and Fig. S3A, most of the possible coexisting substances had almost no quenching effect on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA and N,S-CDs. The exceptions were 1O2 and HOCl, which only slightly influenced the fluorescence of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, but had an obvious influence on the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs, indicating that the covalent linkage of the N,S-CDs to Zn-ICA can improve the specific recognition of NO. To reveal the mechanism, the UV–vis spectra of the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA in the absence and presence of HOCl and NO were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, HOCl can interact with N,S-CDs, which can change the absorption spectra of the N,S-CDs, whereas HOCl does not change the absorption spectra of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, suggesting that N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA may block the formation of ground-state complexes with HOCl, which significantly improves its selectivity for NO. Fig. 3 D and Fig. S3B show the selectivity tests for N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA and N,S-CDs, respectively. The tested possible interferents show almost no effect on the determination of NO except the antioxidants. The antioxidants that are commonly present in organisms will inevitably have some impact on the determination of NO at normal physiological concentrations, which also indirectly indicates that the proposed probe could potentially be applied to evaluate redox homeostasis in vivo. Therefore, the interference from the antioxidants was acceptable. The N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe exhibited better selectivity than the N,S-CDs probe. The selectivity of the former was attributed to its potential ability to block the formation of ground-state complexes with these possible interfering substances, as well as selective recognition via the formation of phenyltriazole between o-phenylenediamine on the surface of the N,S-CDs and NO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA in three types of cells, including two types of tumour cells (MCF-7 and HeLa cells) and normal human bronchial epithelial cells (16HBE cells), was evaluated using a cell proliferation toxicity test (CCK-8). As shown in Fig. 5 S, after 24 h of exposure to different concentrations of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA (0 −500.0 mg/L), the viability of all the cell types was still greater than 85%, indicating that the low-toxicity N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA has some potential in biological applications. A reduction in cell viability is inevitable because of the complex toxicity mechanism of nanoparticles in cells. A cell survival rate of 85% is usually acceptable according to reports in the literatures [47,48]. The concentration of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was 100.0 mg/L, the viability of MCF-7 cells was as high as 90%, indicating that the proposed probe has the advantage of low toxicity. A probe concentration of 100.0 mg/L was chosen for the subsequent cell imaging. In addition, it has been proposed that the metal content and chemical properties of nanoparticles might be more significant factors than particle size and shape in determining their biological toxicity [49]. The toxicity of the probes can be reduced by decreasing the content of the central metal ions during the synthesis of the nanoprobes. The effects of incubation time on the fluorescence imaging and mean fluorescence intensity are shown in Fig. S6. An incubation time of 2 h was selected for subsequent imaging experiments.

For the exogenous exposure of NO in the cells, MCF-7 cells were incubated with the medical-grade NO donor SNP, micromolar concentrations of which can continuously release nanomolar concentrations of NO [50]. As shown in Fig. 4 A b1, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA-loaded MCF-7 cells was significantly decreased after being exposed to 40.0 μM SNP for 30 min. After the culture medium containing SNP was removed, MCF-7 cells were further incubated in fresh culture medium for 6 h or 12 h. As shown in Fig. 4 A c1 and d1, the fluorescence intensity of the cells gradually recovered, which may be attributed to the high concentration (∼5 mM) of GSH and other polypeptide structures containing cysteine residues in the cells, which could react with NO to form nitrosothiols [51]. This indicates that the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe has the potential to monitor dynamic changes in intracellular NO. In addition, because NO serves as an important RNS biomarker and reducing agent in the oxidative stress response of inflammatory cells, LPS (5.0 mg/L) is frequently used to induce the cells and create an oxidative stress model for the fluorescence imaging of endogenous NO in the cells. As shown in Fig. 4 A e1, the red channel fluorescence intensity became significantly weaker with increasing concentrations of endogenous NO. To verify that this change was caused by endogenous NO, the original LPS-induced cells were further treated with 500.0 μM L-NNA, which is an intracellular nitric oxide synthase inhibitor that potently inhibits NO production in the cells [50,52]. As shown in Fig. 4 A f1, the red fluorescence was partially recovered, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA was indeed caused by endogenous NO. Briefly, the red fluorescent probe N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA displayed the dynamic changes in the intracellular NO concentration in real time. The probe is a sensitive and non-invasive tool that can monitor NO fluctuations in living cells, and it can also provide a technical foundation for further understanding the interaction mechanism between oxidative stress and inflammation in cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the different exposure concentrations on intracellular NO levels
ce:section.ce:para: NO is a free radical signalling molecule that is produced by l-arginine through nitric oxide synthase (NOS) in organisms, and i-NOS (inducible nitric oxide synthase), which is expressed after injury, can produce NO at the micromolar level [53]. To date, many studies have shown that NOS is only expressed in malignant breast tissue, such as carcinoma in situ and infiltrating carcinoma, and is not expressed in benign lesions [54]. Thus, MCF-7 cells were chosen as a cell model to verify the imaging ability of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe for NO in vivo and in vitro under the exposure to environmental pollutants. Based on the excellent imaging performance of N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA for endogenous and exogenous NO, we further investigated the changes in intracellular NO in MCF-7 cells under the exposure to different types of pollutants, namely, heavy metal ions, endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs), antibiotics, and other common organic pollutants at different concentrations and exposure times.

It is well known that overexpression of ROS is an important mechanism of the oxidative damage caused by heavy metal ions [55], and that NO is not only a RNS, but also has antioxidant properties and reacts with a variety of ROS to balance the redox system in organisms, which can ultimately prevent the occurrence of diseases [56]. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as the concentration of heavy metal ions was increased in the range of 0 − 10.0 μM. Under this oxidative stress induction, the NO concentration in MCF-7 cells increased in a dose-dependent manner. Among the seven heavy metal ions tested, the content of intracellular NO was the highest under the exposure to 10.0 μΜ Hg2+. However, it is worth noting that when the concentration of heavy metal ions was 100.0 μM, the weakening trend in the intracellular fluorescence intensity slowed, and the fluorescence intensity in MCF-7 cells exposed to Pb2+ and Cd2+ showed a little rebound (Fig. 5 B). Hence, it can be concluded that a high concentration of heavy metal ions exacerbates oxidative damage in MCF-7 cells, and the dramatic increase in ROS leads to a large consumption of intracellular antioxidants [57]. Consequently, the NO content no longer continues to increase, and the intracellular environment is in a state of disordered redox balance. Because of the toxic effect of the heavy metal ions, the MCF-7 cells failed to adhere to the petri dish after 24 h of exposure to 100.0 μΜ of Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-, and no fluorescence could be observed. After the same exposure procedures, the levels of NO2 - induced by heavy metal ions in MCF-7 cells were measured using the traditional Griess standard method. Fig. 5 C shows that when the exposure concentration of heavy metal ions increased, the concentration of NO2 - induced by heavy metal ions also increased gradually, which is consistent with the results of fluorescence imaging (Fig. 5 B). This phenomenon suggests that the real-time NO concentrations in MCF-7 cells were successfully measured using the proposed method, which can provide dynamic monitoring of NO release, unlike the standard Griess assay. The Griess standard method only provides cumulative amounts of NO2 -, as it indirectly measures NO in solution [58]. Therefore, the proposed fluorescent probe with NO specific recognition can provide feasible technical support for assessing dynamic changes in NO during immune/inflammatory responses.

To further investigate the contents of NO induced by different kinds of pollutants in MCF-7 cells, several organic pollutants were selected for comparison with the heavy metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6 B, exposure to the EDCs, such as bisphenol A (BPA), perfluorobutanesulfonate (PFBS), 2,4,6-tribromophenol (TBP), 4-nitrophenol (NP), and estrone (E1), resulted in a similar trend for NO generation in MCF-7 cells, which is consistent with the previous in vitro studies showing that the total NO contents and NOS activity significantly increased in zebrafish embryos exposed to BPA (0.1 −1000 µg/L) and NP (0.1 −100 µg/L) in a dose-dependent manner [59]. 100.0 μΜ NP induced significant apoptosis in MCF-7 cells, so the corresponding fluorescence image could not be observed. Among the other organic pollutants investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 C, exposure to a low concentration (1.0 μΜ) of ciprofloxacin (CIP) may promote the production of ROS in the cells, and causes a corresponding increase in the NO level. At a higher concentration, CIP can induce stronger oxidative damage through activating the mitochondria-ROS pathway in the cells [60], which promotes reaction between large amounts of ROS and NO and causes the restoration of fluorescence. Unlike CIP, the antibiotic tetracycline (TC) induced the production of ROS only at a high concentration (100.0 μΜ), but did not induce intracellular oxidative stress at concentrations of 0 − 10.0 μΜ. Similarly, upon exposure to methylbenzene (MB) and p-nitroaniline (PNA) at concentrations of 0–100.0 μM, the fluorescence intensities in MCF-7 cells showed insignificant changes, suggesting that the concentration of NO in MCF-7 cells did not change significantly, and the specific redox dynamic balance state could not be determined. Additionally, the levels of NO2 - induced by organic pollutants in MCF-7 cells were measured after the same exposure procedures using the traditional Griess standard method. As shown in Figs. 6 D and 6 E, the trends in NO2 - are not completely consistent with those observed in the fluorescence imaging. This is because the Griess standard method only provides the cumulative amount of NO2 - and indirectly measures NO in solution [58]. In comparison with the results for heavy metal ion exposure, the trends in the contents of NO in MCF-7 cells exposed to the organic pollutants seem more complicated, suggesting that the production of ROS induced by the organic pollutants influenced by a wide range of factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the exposure times on intracellular NO levels
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between the exposure time to the various pollutants and the endogenous NO fluctuations in the cells was investigated. To ensure that all cells were alive during the exposure to the various pollutants, an exposure concentration of 10.0 μM was selected. As shown in Fig. 7 A and Fig. 7 B, compared with that of the control groups (0 h), the fluorescence signals of the exposed cells, namely, the mean fluorescence intensity of the red channel in MCF-7 cells, gradually decreased with increasing exposure time, indicating that exposure to a non-lethal concentration of heavy metal ions resulted in a significant increase in NO production in a time-dependent manner. This trend was roughly consistent with the results obtained using the Griess assay (Fig. 7 C). Zhong et al. reported that the production of intracellular NO in MCF-7 cells was inhibited after 4 h of exposure to high concentrations of Pb2+ (1 mM), which may be caused by the down-regulated expression and the activity of nitric oxide synthases (NOSs) [61]. Our study showed that short-term exposure to moderate concentrations of Pb2+ (10 μM) can continuously promote the production of NO and maintain the activity of NOSs. In the 24 h exposure group, the greatest change in fluorescence intensity was induced by Hg2+, while the smallest change in fluorescence intensity was induced by Fe3+. It is reasonable to conclude that the changes in NO production under oxidative stress may be related to the cytotoxicity of the pollutants [62,63]. In addition, the results for the groups exposed to organic pollutants in Fig. 8 A and 8B show that the five exogenous EDCs induced excessive NO production in MCF-7 cells in a time-dependent manner. Compared with the CIP exposure, for generating the similar NO level in MCF-7 cells, E1 exposure needs 24 h, while CIP exposure only needs 12 h. For the other organic pollutants, such as TC and MB, at exposure times longer than 12 h, the fluorescence intensities show a slight recovery, indicating that both of them may rapidly induce the production of ROS and result in an obvious decrease in the NO level, because NO plays an important role in the regulation of redox balance as an antioxidant [64,65]. As shown in Fig. 8 C, the levels of NO2 - induced by organic pollutants in MCF-7 cells were measured using the traditional Griess standard method, and were roughly in line with the results obtained by fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different concentrations of Cd2+ and TBP on the NO levels in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effects of pollutants on oxidative stress in vivo, zebrafish were used. Zebrafish are a classic model for studying various exposure effects such as biocompatibility, toxicity, teratogenicity and immune response, as 99% of their essential genes are conserved in humans [66]. As shown in Fig. 9, with increasing Cd2+ and TBP exposure concentrations, the fluorescence intensities of the zebrafish first decreased and then gradually recovered. TBP can significantly accelerate NO production in zebrafish under oxidative stress at a lower concentration of 0.02 μΜ. A relatively high concentration of Cd2+ (100.0 μΜ) or TBP (0.5 μΜ) causes an obvious decrease in the NO level, as it can promote the production of a large amount of ROS. NO plays an important role as an antioxidant in the regulation of redox balance and leads to the disruption of redox homeostasis in the zebrafish [64,65]. This trend is essentially in agreement with the results of the cell imaging experiments. In addition, it is interesting to note that the luminescence of the merged images was mainly concentrated in the digestive system of the zebrafish, including the liver and intestines, in which the inflammatory reaction may be most evident upon short-term exposure to pollutants. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that the digestive system of the zebrafish is the main site for the metabolism and accumulation of pollutants.

In summary, red-emissive carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and urea as carbon / nitrogen sources, and H2SO4 as sulfur source, which results in red-emissive carbon dots. Glutathione (GSH) was introduced to link the N,S-CDs with metal organic complexes through an amidation reaction to fabricate a metal organic complex fluorescent probe, N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA, which greatly improved the selectivity, stability, and response time of the N,S-CDs. NO reacts specifically with the o-phenylenediamine group to generate a benzotriazole structure, which causes fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe and a blue shift in the emission peak. The composite probe has high selectivity and sensitivity with limit of detection (LOD) of 96.0 nM. The proposed probe was successfully used to monitor dynamic changes in NO levels and evaluate the oxidative stress in MCF-7 cells and zebrafish under the exposure to various pollutants, including seven heavy metal ions and nine organic pollutants, at different concentrations and exposure times. The results showed that short-term exposure to exogenous pollutants induces different degrees of oxidative stress in MCF-7 cells and zebrafish, demonstrating that strong associations exist between the exposure concentration, exposure time, and dynamic changes in NO. Specifically, most heavy metal ions and organic pollutants can induce excessive NO accumulation in cells in a dose- and time-dependent manner. Environmental endocrine disruptors have more pronounced effects than other organic pollutants. The production of NO in living cells and zebrafish exposed to low concentrations of the pollutants is increased, and increasing the exposure concentration accelerates the production of ROS to eliminate NO, which also promotes the process of oxidative stress in vitro and in vivo. It should be pointed out that only monitoring NO for evaluating oxidative stress induced by pollutants in vitro and in vivo is not enough, and multiple indicators should be detected. The proposed N,S-CDs@Zn-ICA probe has potential application for real-time monitoring of fluctuations in endogenous NO and provides further insights into evaluating the health risks of environmental pollutants to organisms.

Endogenous nitric oxide (NO) as an active free radical is an important gas signalling molecule in organisms, which is ubiquitous in human immune, nervous and cardiovascular systems. Hence, it is very necessary to develop a highly selective and sensitive method for real-time monitoring of the intracellular NO fluctuation in vivo so that clarify the biological mechanism of pollutants-induced oxidative damage. Furthermore, it is quite important that the individual species of ROS was detected and evaluated under the exposure to pollutants, which can further understand the production and mechanism of oxidative stress.

Meng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Changlun Tong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
